## Time-stamped data needs explicit date handling

Many datasets in `EDI 3400` include a date or timestamp column. When Pandas reads
that column as plain text, date-based operations like filtering by month or
computing weekly averages do not work correctly.

The fix is to tell Pandas that the column contains dates:

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("daily_campus_energy.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
```

The `parse_dates` argument converts the named column from strings into proper
`datetime` objects. This one step unlocks all of Pandas' date-aware features.

## A DatetimeIndex turns dates into the primary axis

Once the date column is parsed, setting it as the index makes the entire table
date-indexed:

```python
df = df.set_index("date")
print(df.index)
```

A `DatetimeIndex` supports:

- slicing by date range: `df.loc["2025-01":"2025-03"]`,
- grouping by calendar period,
- and resampling to different time resolutions.

The `pd.to_datetime()` function is the manual equivalent when `parse_dates` is
not enough:

```python
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"], format="%Y-%m-%d")
```

This is useful when the date format is non-standard or when the column needs
cleaning before conversion.

## Resampling changes the time resolution of a table

Daily data is often too granular for summary analysis. Resampling converts a
time-indexed table to a coarser resolution:

```python
monthly = df.resample("ME").mean()
print(monthly.head())
```

The `"ME"` rule means "month end" — each row in the result represents one
calendar month. The `.mean()` aggregation computes the average of all daily
values within each month.

Other common rules:

- `"W"` for weekly,
- `"QE"` for quarterly,
- `"YE"` for yearly.

Resampling is a form of downsampling: it reduces the number of rows while
preserving the overall trend. This is the time-series equivalent of the grouping
and aggregation students already know from Pandas.

## Time-series plots reveal patterns that summary statistics miss

A single mean or standard deviation hides the shape of the data over time.
Plotting the values on a time axis makes trends, cycles, and outliers visible:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df["kwh"].plot(title="Daily campus energy use")
plt.ylabel("kWh")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Pandas integrates directly with Matplotlib through the `.plot()` method. When
the index is a `DatetimeIndex`, the x-axis automatically shows dates.

Comparing the daily plot to a monthly resampled plot is a good way to see how
aggregation smooths out short-term noise while preserving the underlying trend.

::: {.teacher-only}
## Live demo suggestion

Show a daily energy or temperature plot first, then overlay the monthly
resampled version on the same axes. This makes the smoothing effect concrete.
For a more engaging example, use a stock price CSV if the class is comfortable
with financial data.
:::

## Rolling windows smooth short-term noise

Resampling groups data into fixed calendar periods. Rolling windows instead
compute a statistic over a sliding window of fixed width:

```python
df["rolling_7d"] = df["kwh"].rolling(window=7).mean()
df[["kwh", "rolling_7d"]].plot(title="Daily energy with 7-day rolling average")
plt.ylabel("kWh")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

A 7-day rolling mean smooths out day-to-day variation while keeping the original
daily resolution. The first six rows will be `NaN` because the window needs at
least seven values to compute the first average.

Rolling windows are useful when:

- the data has a clear daily or weekly cycle,
- students want to see the trend without losing the original time resolution,
- and the goal is visual clarity rather than formal statistical modeling.

## What students should be able to do next

After this concept, students should be able to:

- load a CSV file with `parse_dates` and set a date column as the index,
- explain the difference between a plain index and a `DatetimeIndex`,
- resample a daily table to monthly or weekly resolution,
- plot a time series with `.plot()` and appropriate labels,
- compute a rolling average and explain why the first few values are `NaN`.
